title: Monetary Policy Report—July 2024
summary: The Bank of Canada reduced its target for the overnight rate by 25 basis points to 4½% on July 24, 2024 (per context), marking the first rate cut in the current cycle. Core inflation is expected to ease to about 2½% in H2 2024 and return sustainably to the 2% target later in 2025. The Bank considers risks to its inflation outlook to be balanced, with upside risks including persistent services price inflation and downside risks including weaker household spending.
key_points: Bank of Canada cut the overnight rate target by 25 bps to 4.5% (Bank Rate 4.75%, deposit rate 4.5%), continuing balance sheet normalization.
Core inflation expected to ease to approximately 2.5% in the second half of 2024.
CPI inflation forecast to temporarily fall below core inflation (due to gasoline prices), then rise modestly into H1 2025 before returning to 2% target later that year.
Labour market has cooled significantly; strong population growth added to worker supply, creating excess supply expected to pull inflation down.
Wage growth remains elevated; services inflation most affected by wages is proving persistent.
Rent and mortgage interest costs continue to keep shelter price inflation high.
Global economy expected to grow at around 3% over the projection horizon; US growth has slowed while euro area is expanding after stalling in 2023.
why_it_matters: This report provides the Bank of Canada's official assessment of the Canadian economy and signals the beginning of an easing cycle after prolonged rate hikes. The balance of risks being deemed 'balanced' suggests the Bank sees symmetric uncertainty around its inflation forecast, leaving room for further rate cuts if conditions warrant. The focus on services inflation persistence and shelter costs highlights the most challenging components for achieving the 2% target.
macro_context: The Bank shifted to an easing stance with this cut, but emphasized that inflation progress will be uneven and some pressures, particularly services and shelter, remain sticky. The forecast shows CPI temporarily dipping below core inflation due to energy prices before rebounding, suggesting a choppy disinflation path. The global backdrop shows US slowing and euro area stabilizing, with inflation still above central bank targets in both regions.
market_structure_context: The Bank is continuing its policy of balance sheet normalization. No changes to the operating framework or tools were mentioned in the excerpt.
